attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman, ex-competitive swimmer, presents to her GP with a several month history of increasing lethargy. She struggles to engage in activities of daily living and does not have the energy to engage in much physical activity. When she has tried to exercise, she has felt too tired and had to call in sick at work for the next two days. In addition to difficulty attending work, she reports poor concentration. She does not feel refreshed when she wakes in the morning. She is dizzy on standing. She feels she has gained weight recently. Whilst the lethargy has been a concern for her, she still tries to see her friends regularly for coffee each week as this brings her joy. She denies any triggers or trauma. She has no medical conditions and takes no regular medications.

On examination, her postural blood pressure is normal. There is no hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. Her body mass index is 27kg/m2.

Previous investigations show very mildly elevated ESR and CRP. Other routine blood tests including a CBE, EUC, LFT, TFT, iron studies, HbA1c, vitamin D, and lipid profile were all normal. Tests for Epstein Barr Virus (VCA IgM and IgG and EBNA) are negative.
attributes.stemQuestion: Given the most likely diagnosis, which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Ensure they meet the standard exercise and physical activity guidelines
Individualised physical activity and sleep hygiene
Consolidate care solely to the GP for patient ease
attributes.explanation:
The most likely diagnosis is myalgic encephalitis / chronic fatigue syndrome (ME/CFS). The combination of ongoing fatigue with normal screening blood results could also hint at depression though this is less likely when she still enjoys seeing friends. The history of attempting rigorous exercise that causes increased fatigue is also more typical of ME/CFS.

Individualised physical activity and sleep hygiene is the most appropriate response. In ME/CFS a patient's symptoms are not static, and flare-ups and relapses are a common part of the disease process. As such, having a consistent regimen is not always possible. Flares can be caused by over-exertion, acute illness, increased stress, or with no clear trigger. In a relapse where symptoms are worsened for a prolonged period of time, reduction of activity and increases in rest are often required. Management of ME/CFS can be difficult and the capacity and ability of the patient can require frequent re-assessment to try and maintain optimal health. Thus, having an individualised and flexible routine is important in balancing the benefits exercise and sleep offer with other aspects of life and external stressors.

Engage in cognitive based therapy is the not the most appropriate response. CBT can play a role but the benefits are unclear. Whilst not all patients may be open to the idea, CBT can play a role in helping manage symptoms and adapting to life with a chronic illness. Key aspects include discussing challenges they face, developing a self-management plan, and identifying strategies to improve function and day-to-day life. It should only be offered to support people who live with ME/CFS to manage their symptoms, improve their functioning and reduce the distress associated with having a chronic illness.

Ensure they meet the standard exercise and physical activity guidelines is not the most appropriate response. These are the standard recommendations for physical activity for adults. People with MC/CFS should have an exercise regimen designed and overseen by their treatment team which should include both medical specialists and allied health, but especially an exercise physiologist to help design a graded and well-tolerated activity program. People with ME/CFS may inadvertently worsen their symptoms if they do too much.

Attempt to "push through" their limitations is not the most appropriate response. A graded approach to exercise and physical activity is recommended in ME/CFS. Attempting to "push through" their symptoms may result in a flare of their symptoms, causing worsened fatigue and having broader impacts on their ability to engage in their ADLs.

Consolidate care solely to the GP for patient ease is not the most appropriate response. Having a good GP is very important in the management of a patient with a complex condition such as ME/CFS. However, their care should involve a large team of experts, including rheumatology, endocrinology, rehabilitation medicine, neurology, physiotherapy, dietetics, occupational therapy, exercise physiology, and psychology. It is unreasonable to expect a single health care provider to fill the role of many experts, particularly in a condition that has such broad impacts.
learningPoints: Management of ME/CFS requires a multidisciplinary approach to achieve best patient outcomes.
references: NICE (2021). Myalgic encephalomyelitis (or encephalopathy)/chronic fatigue syndrome: diagnosis and management. https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng206
